entities.description: A 2003 electronic selection from Warner's 1889 edition. It encodes the Egerton-base English text and English notes, omits the parallel French text and front and back matter, and supplies pointers to U-M's Cotton-Version divisions.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item acd9576
notes: The item header was created by hand from the text and a MARC record of a microfilm copy on 26 March 2003; no item-specific production statement identifies keying, OCR, or correction methods.
The language declaration names Latin and Greek but omits overwhelmingly primary Middle English.
The selected XML contains 580 NOTE1 elements, most of them Warner's English footnotes, and 56 SUPPLIED spans for omissions or damage.
Thirty-four Greek alphabet nodes contain GAP rather than the printed glyphs.

bibliographic.citation: George F. Warner, ed., The Buke of John Maundeuill, Being the Travels of Sir John Mandeville, Knight, 1322-1356: A Hitherto Unpublished English Version from the Unique Copy (Egerton MS. 1982) in the British Museum, Roxburghe Club Publications 119 (Westminster: Nichols and Sons, 1889).
bibliographic.title: The Buke of John Maundeuill

entities.description: A large-paper bilingual scholarly edition: the Egerton English version and a reconstructed French source text appear in parallel on pp. 1-156, followed by commentary and index; the volume also contains an introduction, glossary, and twenty-eight manuscript-miniature plates. Warner used Egerton MS 1982 as the English base, Cotton Titus C XVI for English headings, explicit supplies, and variants, Harley MS 4383 and Royal MS 20 B X for the French base, and Sloane MS 1464 and Grenville XXXIX for French variants.
entities.label: Warner's 1889 edition, The Buke of John Maundeuill
notes: The title's 1322-1356 dates follow the common version; the Egerton prologue instead claims departure in 1332.
Warner's French text is subsidiary source reconstruction and apparatus, not a second work selected by U-M.
Warner moved an Egerton-only passage about Thule and Saint Thomas of Canterbury from the running text to commentary p. 220.

rights.basis: The edition was first published in 1889, U-M currently labels its selected materials public domain in the United States, and Internet Archive applies the Public Domain Mark to the complete scan. Copyright Office Circular 15A describes a 95-year maximum term for works already under statutory protection before 1978; the publication is now more than 95 years old.
rights.component: Warner's 1889 transcription, French text, introduction, apparatus, commentary, index, and published plates

entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of the Egerton Middle English version. Warner describes an undivided text copied in one northern hand on 129 leaves; modern scholarship dates the manuscript around 1400. Marginal place names are irregular or misplaced and were omitted from Warner's running edition.

holding.notes: Held at the British Library, London; current record offers physical request but no digitized content.

entities.label: British Library Egerton MS 1982

physicalDescription.extent: 129 leaves according to Warner; the encoded work has intended folio-side markers from approximately 3r to 132v
notes: Warner reports one hand and no chapter divisions.

The British Library records flyleaf provenance claims involving St Albans, Caxton, Tottel, Tuthill, and Hill; Warner explicitly doubted the Caxton claim because its alleged supporting note was lost, so these are not treated as established transmission facts.

entities.description: The abstract Middle English version represented uniquely by Egerton MS 1982. Seymour describes it as conflating a lost Defective-Version manuscript and a lost Middle English translation of the Anglo-Latin Royal Version.
entities.label: The Egerton Version of the Book of John Mandeville

entities.description: A reported lost manuscript or textual intermediary of the Defective Version posited by Seymour as one source strand behind the Egerton Version; no physical exemplar is identified.
entities.label: Lost Defective-Version source behind the Egerton Version

entities.description: A reported lost Middle English translation posited by Seymour as the second source strand behind the Egerton Version; no physical exemplar is identified.
entities.label: Lost Middle English translation of the Anglo-Latin Royal Version

entities.description: An Anglo-Latin version tradition represented indirectly in the Egerton Version through a posited lost Middle English translation.
entities.label: The Anglo-Latin Royal Version of the Book of John Mandeville

entities.description: The anonymous or pseudonymous fourteenth-century multilingual compilation and multiversion tradition of which the Egerton, Cotton, Defective, and Anglo-Latin Royal versions are manifestations.
entities.label: The Book of John Mandeville

reviewNotes: The cataloged intellectual work is the distinct Middle English Egerton Version, not the physical codex, the multilingual Book of John Mandeville, Warner's bilingual volume, or U-M's digital selection.
The unique Egerton witness is undivided. Warner imposed thirty-four chapters and headings from Cotton, closely following the Harley/Royal French text; U-M serializes those editorial divisions.
French is present in Warner's source and apparatus layers but was explicitly omitted from U-M and is not treated as a primary language of the selected Egerton text.
Cotton contributed headings, five clearly identifiable supplied passages, and variants. The XML's fifty-six SUPPLIED spans are not all Cotton-derived.
The current U-M No Copyright-United States statement and the restrictive embedded 2003 availability notice are preserved as separate claims rather than silently reconciled.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan CME item acd9576, a 2003 digital selection encoded from George F. Warner's 1889 Roxburghe Club edition. Warner transcribed the unique Egerton MS 1982 English version, supplied chapter headings and several passages from Cotton MS Titus C XVI, collated Cotton variants, and printed a French source text in parallel from Harley MS 4383 and Royal MS 20 B X with apparatus from Sloane MS 1464 and Grenville XXXIX, now Additional MS 33757. U-M retained the English text and its extensive English footnotes but explicitly omitted the French stream and the edition's front and back matter and plates; it added CORRESP pointers to the Cotton encoding and moved an Egerton-only passage from Warner's commentary into the body. A complete public scan of the 1889 edition is available through Internet Archive, but no current complete public facsimile was verified for any manuscript witness.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator-redactor; traditionally attributed to the fictional or legendary narrator John Mandeville
catalogSummary.date: Around 1400; uniquely attested in Egerton MS 1982
catalogSummary.region: England; the unique witness's scribal language is localized to the North Riding of Yorkshire
catalogSummary.title: The Egerton Version of Mandeville's Travels

catalogingSubject.label: The Egerton Version of Mandeville's Travels
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The distinct anonymous Middle English Egerton Version of the Book of John Mandeville, uniquely attested in British Library Egerton MS 1982. This record does not describe the abstract multilingual Book of John Mandeville, the physical codex as a whole, the Cotton Version, Warner's bilingual 1889 volume, the University of Michigan selection, or the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: The Egerton Version of Mandeville's Travels

locators.value: English text pp. 1-156; one Egerton-only passage printed in commentary p. 220

notes: Warner's and U-M's chapter divisions are useful access points but do not warrant thirty-four intrinsic content parts in a work-level manifest.
contentParts.summary: The complete continuous Egerton-version prose, from its pilgrimage prologue through routes to Jerusalem, descriptions of Asia and marvels, cosmographical reflections, and the narrator's closing appeal for prayer.

notes: The unique witness is dated around 1400 in modern scholarship. That date constrains the attested version but does not prove that translation, redaction, and copying were one event.
The Egerton Version is uniquely attested in this witness.

The Egerton prologue says the narrator departed in 1332; the resulting narrated travel span is not a composition date or verified biography. French, Latin, Cotton, and several other versions instead give 1322, and Warner's long title uses 1322-1356.

extent.description: One continuous prose work. The U-M representation contains a prologue and thirty-four editor-imposed chapter DIV1 elements, 214 body paragraphs, print-page milestones for Warner pp. 1-156, and intended folio-side milestones spanning approximately Egerton fols. 3r-132v. Its 580 NOTE1 elements and 56 SUPPLIED elements are nested editorial apparatus or restorations and are excluded from structural counts. The thirty-four chapters are not intrinsic manuscript sections.

formStatements.description: A continuous prose compilation with embedded quotations. The thirty-four numbered chapters in Warner and U-M are editorial divisions imposed on an undivided manuscript.

genres.label: Travel narrative
notes: The first-person itinerary is a literary compilation and should not be cataloged as verified eyewitness travel.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The U-M header omits the primary language; direct inspection of the encoded work establishes Middle English.

scope.description: Quotations, titles, formulas, and individual expressions embedded in the Middle English prose

languageStatements.label: Ancient Greek
notes: Thirty-four encoded Greek FOREIGN nodes contain GAP rather than glyphs; four other Greek-language quotations are transliterated. Warner's French parallel is an edition layer and is not a co-primary language of this Middle English version.

scope.description: A small number of transliterated quotations and an alphabet passage whose Greek glyphs are absent from the XML

notes: The U-M header lists Latin and Greek but omits primary Middle English; language statements were derived from direct text inspection rather than copied mechanically from the header.

Warner's French parallel is a source-edition and apparatus layer. Because U-M explicitly omitted it, French is not treated as a language of the cataloged Egerton Middle English work or its selected digital text.
The manuscript's lack of divisions prevents Warner's thirty-four Cotton-derived chapter headings from being counted as intrinsic work structure.
Copyright, access, local source-cache delivery, and the conflict between U-M's current public-domain statement and its embedded redistribution restriction are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.

next_steps: Consult Seymour's full 2010 introduction and subsequent version scholarship.
Keep the c. 1400 witness date and North Riding scribal localization separate from an unproven redaction date and place.
openQuestions.question: When and where was the Egerton Version created, and which distinctive readings belong to its translator-redactor rather than the sole surviving scribe?

next_steps: Review Seymour's full textual apparatus and later stemmatic research.
Retain the lost intermediaries as reported or hypothesized entities, not identified physical exemplars.
openQuestions.question: Can the two lost source strands posited for the Egerton Version be related more precisely to extant Defective and Anglo-Latin Royal witnesses?

next_steps: Compare the passage in Seymour's edition and apparatus with the Egerton manuscript and wider Mandeville traditions.
Preserve Warner's and U-M's different placements in lineage rather than selecting one silently.
openQuestions.question: What is the textual status and origin of the Egerton-only Thule and Saint Thomas of Canterbury passage that Warner removed to commentary and U-M restored to the body?

notes: This is an English-language version in the English manuscript tradition; no precise place of redaction is established.
LALME/MEG-C localizes the witness sample to the North Riding. This is not treated as proof that the translator-redactor worked there.

scope.description: Scribal language of the unique Egerton witness

notes: The Egerton Version combines material associated with the Defective tradition and the Anglo-Latin Royal tradition. No reviewed source identifies the Middle English translator-redactor.
Adaptation distinguishes the Egerton Version's conflation and rearrangement from a simple word-for-word translation claim.
The prologue names an English knight from St Albans as first-person narrator, but the persona is legendary or pseudonymous and does not establish a historical author's identity.
Witness-level copying is kept distinct from responsibility for creating the intellectual version.

scope.description: Copying of the unique surviving Egerton witness
Egerton-base English text and Harley/Royal-base French parallel text
Cotton English variants and supplies and French-witness apparatus
Introduction, footnotes, glossary, commentary, and index in the 1889 edition

subjects.label: Routes to Jerusalem, sacred geography, pilgrimage sites, and relics
The Eastern Mediterranean, Near East, Central Asia, and India
Christianities, Islam, Judaism, other religions, and reported customs
notes: Descriptions reflect medieval Christian polemic and inherited literary sources, not neutral ethnography.

subjects.label: Marvelous peoples, animals, islands, and natural wonders
Cosmography, the round earth, geography, and antipodes
The Great Khan, Prester John, and imagined Eastern polities

summaries.value: A northern Middle English version of the Book of John Mandeville, framed as the recollections and guidance of an English knight who claims to have travelled from St Albans through the Holy Land and onward across Asia. The work combines pilgrimage routes and sacred sites with geography, religions, political powers, natural history, cosmography, and marvels. It is an anonymous literary compilation and adaptation of inherited authorities and version traditions, not reliable evidence of the narrator's claimed eyewitness journey.

The unique Egerton manuscript has no chapter divisions. Warner divided its English text into thirty-four chapters using Cotton headings closely related to the French Harley and Royal witnesses, printed a French source text in parallel, and added extensive apparatus and commentary. U-M selected Warner's English text and footnotes, omitted the entire French stream and front and back matter, linked its editorial divisions to the Cotton encoding through CORRESP, and inserted into the body an Egerton-only passage that Warner had placed in commentary.

titles.value: The Egerton Version of Mandeville's Travels

notes: The manuscript heading is preserved in Warner's transcription and the U-M encoding; capitalization is normalized here from display capitals.
titles.value: Here begynnez þe buke of John Maundeuill

notes: Short title of Warner's 1889 edition; the edition's long subtitle also calls the text a hitherto unpublished English version from Egerton MS 1982.

work.author: Anonymous Middle English translator-redactor; traditionally attributed to the fictional or legendary narrator John Mandeville
work.dateDisplay: Around 1400; uniquely attested in Egerton MS 1982
